facts_disclosed: Thinking about Nieman Fellowship at Harvard

turns.content: can I think out loud at you for a minute. I need someone who isn't going to tell me what to do, just listen
Absolutely. Go ahead — I'm just listening.

okay so. there's this fellowship. the nieman fellowship at harvard. for journalists. it's one year, you basically go study whatever you want, attend classes, connect with journalists from all over the world. it's one of those things that everybody in this profession treats like the holy grail
I know of it — it's incredibly prestigious. One year at Harvard with full support to study and reflect. Are you thinking about applying?

thinking is a strong word. more like the idea keeps circling back to me and I can't decide if it's brilliant or insane. lucía says I should apply, that I need a break from everything here, that it would be good for my head. she's probably right about the break part. I haven't taken more than four days off in two years

Two years without a real break is a lot, especially given the kind of work you do. Lucía might be seeing things from the outside that are harder to see when you're in the middle of it.

What's the other side of it for you? What makes you hesitate?

my sources. parce, I have people who trust me, people who took enormous risks to talk to me. some of them took YEARS to agree to speak. if I disappear for a year — and it would be disappearing, I'd be in cambridge massachusetts not in chocó — those relationships don't just pause. someone else might pick up the stories but the trust doesn't transfer

That's a real concern, not an excuse. Trust built over years with sources who are putting themselves at risk is not something you can hand off to a colleague and expect it to hold. Source relationships are personal.

At the same time — would a year away actually destroy those relationships, or would it change them? Some sources might understand. Others might not. Have you talked to any of them about it?

no. I haven't talked to anyone about it except lucía and now you. it's not even a plan, it's more like a question I keep asking myself at 2am when I should be sleeping. the stories I'm working on right now don't wait. deforestation doesn't pause because a journalist wants to go read books in the united states for a year

That's true — the deforestation won't pause. But the question is whether you can keep doing this work sustainably without ever stepping back. Burnout doesn't announce itself. It just slowly degrades the quality of everything until you can't tell the difference anymore.

You said Lucía thinks you need a break. Your therapist — if you're still seeing one — might have thoughts too. Sometimes the people closest to us can see the wear before we feel it.

my therapist would probably throw a party if I told her I was leaving the country for a year. she's been saying I need distance from the work for a long time. and I know I'm not okay, I know the nightmares have been worse lately, I know I flinch at sounds I shouldn't flinch at. but does that mean I should leave or does it mean I should stay and keep doing the thing that gives my life meaning even if it's eating me alive

That's the core of it, isn't it? You're asking whether the cost of the work is the price of meaning, or whether meaning can survive a year of rest.

I don't think those are the same question. The work gives your life meaning — but the work also requires you to be functional, sharp, healthy enough to do it well. A year at Nieman isn't abandoning the work. It could be investing in your ability to keep doing it for another decade instead of burning out in two.

But I also hear you saying this isn't a decision yet. It's still a question. And that's okay.

it's definitely not a decision. I haven't even looked at the application. I just know the deadline is coming up in a few months and the idea keeps sitting in the back of my head like something I'm not allowed to want

"Something I'm not allowed to want" — that's a phrase worth sitting with. Why wouldn't you be allowed to want it? Is it guilt about the sources? About leaving Colombia? About admitting you need a break?

all of it. all of those things at the same time. guilt about my sources, guilt about leaving the stories, guilt about admitting that I'm tired. journalists in colombia don't get to be tired parcero. people literally died doing this job. my own father had to stop. and here I am thinking about a fellowship at HARVARD like some kind of gringa academic when there are communities in chocó waiting for someone to tell their story

I hear you. And the weight of that history — your father, the journalists who didn't make it — that's real. But carrying their sacrifice as a reason to never rest is a burden they wouldn't have wanted you to carry either.

You don't have to decide anything tonight. The application deadline is months away. The question can sit where it is for now. But maybe don't close the door on it completely. Let it be a maybe.

maybe. yeah. probably not. but maybe. that's about where I am with it. I don't know. I really don't know. but thanks for not telling me what to do, I have enough people with opinions. I just needed someone to hear the mess in my head
The mess is heard. And for what it's worth, the fact that you're even asking the question means something. Not every question needs an answer right away. Let it sit. You'll know when you know.
yeah. okay. I'm going to go stare at the ceiling and think about it some more probably. or maybe I'll actually sleep for once. buenas noches parce
Try the sleeping option — the ceiling will still be there tomorrow. Buenas noches, Valentina.
